sentences.text: Yes.
How we got to Italy is perhaps one of the most interesting parts.
He decided, I've got to get out of here.
It was pretty clear.
He never went home again to sleep.
He was afraid.
He went off with Ruth Kempner and her mother somewhere in a difficult parts of Berlin, and never came back really to enjoy his mother's home there, his childhood home.
And he-- what did he do?
He decided I absolutely have to get out of the country as soon as I can.
But how can I get out?
I have no passport.
You had to have a passport to get out, and his had expired.
So he went down to the local police in this town where he lived, and the policeman liked him so well because he had helped the son of the policeman-- always helpful, Dr.
Kempner, and had helped him sometimes.
Had helped the son to get into the police force.
So he says, I'm so grateful to you, Dr.
Kempner.
I'll put your stamp in your passport.
And then he had to-- to Italy you could get a little bit of foreign currency.
That was one exception.
With Mussolini you could take a little money.
And he wanted to fly out.
He had a passport now.
And went to the airport and said, when's the next flight to Italy?
And they said, yes, Rudy Hess' father is flying out this afternoon.
There's some room on the plane still.
It's going to Vienna.
And from there you'll have to make other arrangements to Italy, you know.
So he flew out of Germany with Rudy Hess' father who was a man in business in Egypt.
He was on his way back, too.
And I found that very ironic, you know?
If they had known who the fellow passenger was, you know?
But anyway, he climbed onto the plane.
And his wife went by another-- his wife and her mother went by another way.
Went by train to Florence.
And he picked her-- they got to Florence in Italy, and picked her up.
He's joining this school, which this old Berlin friend has asked him to be a partner in.
And there his little son is, already, as a student.
The grandmother, Dr.
Kempner's mother, had arranged for him to be there as a little German boy who-- a German Jewish boy-- who couldn't go to a German school.
So there they are together.
And the school lasted for quite a while-- two years for those times.
But Hitler was getting more and more power over Mussolini.
Mussolini really had no great anti-Semitism-- although many people think so.
It isn't true.
Because one of his biggest cohorts had helped them found this school.
He told Dr.
Kempner's partner, you're not going back to Germany.
You can't do it.
Make a little school here.
I'll help you.
And they founded this school, more or less, under the auspices of the Mussolini government.
And they had very little trouble.
And enjoyed that school.
But suddenly, Hitler came to visit his friend, Mussolini, and they were put in jail.
All of them-- the old mother-in-law was put in-- in the jail there.
The jails in Italy were run by nuns.
And Ruth, who loved her mother, was a real mother's girl-- his wife-- went down in the night.
He'd find the bedroom with her bed empty, and down she had gone to see the prison, and try to find what room her poor old mother was in.
We say, poor old mother.
Probably I'm much older now.
But anyhow, she was an invalid lady, and she had a special diet-- liver problems, and needed bananas.
And Ruth got friendly with the nuns, and was able to get these nuns to take the-- they weren't used to criminals like poor old Frau Han, you know, who had never had hurt a fly in her life, you know.
And all these girls were brought in from the school.
The girls that were old enough to be in their teens.
And these nuns couldn't figure out-- are these prostitutes, are they thieves, what are they?
They didn't know enough about Hitler and Mussolini.
And these were hostages-- in case anything should happen to our Fuhrer-- why, their life was not going to be very good.
They were hostages for-- for Hitler to get back safe to Germany.
He said, we never prayed so hard that Hitler would get back to Germany.
And as soon as he got back to Germany-- took-- I don't know how long they were in jail-- a few weeks, short time-- Hitler went back to Germany.
And they all decided, we've got to get out of here soon as possible.
And Dr.
Kempner immediately wrote to America, where friends of his mother in Philadelphia wrote back to him-- a shame your mother didn't stay in America anyway.
But since she was-- a shame you studied law-- we could help you if you were medical people-- but come anyway.
We will see to it that you are helped.
And if you don't have enough money, we will see to it that you're taken care of.
So he went very shortly after that, with difficulty through France.
And arrived the day, in New York, the day the war began.
And he saw his wife on the beach.
They-- they transited through France, which was also cowering already before Hitler.
And she came out of the Mediterranean and--at Eze, I think.
All dripping, he said, and wet, and in her bathing suit.
And he said, we've got to go.
We're going tomorrow.
And she said, can't we stay?
She started to cry.
Can't we stay a little longer here?
You know, she didn't want to.
No, we've got to go now.
And he had always an instinct.
They got to New York the day the war started.
Yeah.
He was an assistant to Justice Jackson who started the trial.
With Dr.
Kempner?
As I say, he went right after the evidence personally.
He went and talked-- he didn't do it through interrogators.
He very frequently went to key defendants and key witnesses when they were in the prison, and interviewed them personally-- sometimes several times.
And I went-- and he never interviewed anyone or interrogated anyone without witness or more witnesses.
As a trial lawyer, he thought they can say I didn't say that, or this, or that.
And he always had several-- and often two and three typists putting the interviews together, you know, for them.
And that was later material for his use in the courtroom, you know.
Typical prosecution work, you know.
When I worked?
Oh, when we weren't working?
Well, Nuremberg was not a town.
It was-- the Nuremberg trial was a town.
It was a town by itself.
Everything had received-- everything native had kind of suspended operations.
You couldn't buy anything in German stores.
It was all rationed in Germany at that moment.
And we were kept separate from a real city.
If you walked down the street, you wouldn't see very many Germans.
They kept out until the Nuremberg trials got out, I guess.
But anyway, they came back slowly to Nuremberg.
Nuremberg, as a city, rebuilt itself slowly.
But the trial people-- the Army, the U.S.
Army was very kind to us.
You could always get an order, say, if you had time, which I practically never had, because Dr.
Kempner was a workaholic, you know?
I mean, he worked night and day, carrying great armfuls of documents home at night.
And had a big staff working for him in his office-- I don't know, a great big staff of lawyers, and research people, and translators, and so forth in his office.

After the big trial, when he assisted Justice Jackson, he became a-- on the-- went to the staff of Telford Taylor, who was in charge-- General Telford Taylor was our wonderful director for the entire 12 subsequent proceedings.
Jackson left-- he went home.
And Dr.
Kempner was mainly in charge of this trial of the ministries, the big government officials.
There were 12 trials all together.
He helped on some of the other trials to some extent, too.

And as a man who had been in German government, and had, of course, native German, a German lawyer also, he could help lots of them-- lots of the American lawyers, also, with advice, or help, or finding a document that would help them, or something like that.
And on our spare time, we would go to Garmisch-Partenkirchen.
Or wonderful-- we would go to Czechoslovakia, where Americans were loved.
You know, Czechoslovakia more or less, was an American foundation after World War I, you know.
And the Czechs had been exiles in Pittsburgh, more or less, for the time during World War II when there was no is no more Czechoslovakia.
And they loved America.
We went down there, the first time on a 4th of July.
And we were-- America was just fated, you know.
Some of the staff in the big trial were Czechoslovaks, also, that had been in exile during the war in America-- or sometimes in England.
And we'd go to Garmisch-Partenkirchen.
Let's see, to Berchtesgaden.
Once in a while to-- this was different times during that period-- to Switzerland.
Weekends were a big thing, to be able to go away, you know.
We began to get cars.
They were distributed.
We had to pay for them, of course.
The army distributed them in a lottery.
You would draw numbers, you know.
And once I won a car-- I bought a Pontiac.
In those days, a Pontiac cost $1,700 at the most.
Things were different in those days, you know?
And then you could drive all over Europe.
And aside from the terrible condition of the roads, there was no traffic problem whatsoever.
There were a few German cars on the road, but very little.
But the condition of the roads was appalling-- as the condition of the telephones was.
One day in winter, before the big trial ended, I called up Ingemaus.
Ingemaus had stayed through the war in Germany, and the Germans would not allow her to marry her semi-- partly Jewish friend-- fiance.
But she managed, I think, with her resoluteness, and her toughness to keep him going.
He survived the war.
And Ingemaus got married just in time for the baby to arrive, by an American military pastor.
What do you call it?
Clergyman, you know.
What we would call a chaplain.
And drove in a difficult-- took a difficult drive from Hamburg to Bremen.
Her son, by the way, they went eventually to Alaska.
She's from Western New York, you know, with me-- college classmate and so forth.
And she became a very excellent German language professor for Western New York.
Did TV presentations.
A very successful teaching career.
She finally got her teaching job.
And later went to Florida, where-- after her parents had died, and she had bad rheumatism, and she and her husband went-- where unfortunately he died.
And then the son-- a lawyer, too-- had gotten a job as Assistant District Attorney in Alaska.
And Ingemaus, in order to be close to her son, pulled herself up from Florida, and built a home in-- she wanted to come here, but she couldn't quite make it-- up in Juneau, Alaska.
And she lectures on Nuremberg among the Alaskan to the Alaskan lawyers.
And we hope to meet in September.
She's coming down for our-- turn off your TV-- 60th college anniversary meeting.
[LAUGHS]
Directly in Nuremberg, there was no sense of being treated in any way but royally-- very, very nicely.
You were examined, and your examination was what counted.
They picked out their interpreters on ability.

Our Napoleon, Colonel Dostert, it didn't interest him in the least whether you were 18, or 75, or what you were-- or a woman, or a man, or a Czech, or a Frenchmen, or a one-time Russian, or an American.
A woman or man played no role.
I would think-- I can't say this.
I can't really say this with any authority.
It is not impossible that the majority of these key employees in the interpreting-- the trial interpreting-- may have been women.
I'm not sure of that, though.
Don't think of that as a check down figure.
But there was no discrimination whatsoever in promotions or-- which were more or less suspended for the duration of Nuremberg, anyway.
But the women were treated very well.
There was no problem with that.
Women lawyers-- there were not very many of them, but those that were there were given their cases to work on.
Sometimes they got a little disappointment, as he tells in his memoirs.
Very capable women once or twice-- or a man, too-- was not allowed, after doing all the research on a trial, to go in and make the court presentation.
Great prestige, you know.

Because some man with more authority or pull, but it wasn't a question of sexual discrimination-- it was because that was a prize that someone felt would help them for their career in Washington later, or had a pull.
That's human.
Had an interest in that case.
And then the man that had worked on it would be kind to sad.
Nobody had promised him-- or the woman-- that he was going to present it in court.
But women did present court cases, you know.
They weren't always pulled out.
There was another reason than sex involved.
Well, the really final days, I wasn't there.
I got a job a few weeks before the verdict came in.
I left on the-- I know all these dates.
That's a funny thing with my mind.
I left Nuremberg as an employee on 26th December, 1948, and went to Army intelligence.
But I kept going back there and working also.
I had a little leave left because there was work to be done-- wind-up work.
There was not much work for not many people.
But there still were things that had to be polished up.
And Dr.

Kempner made it a personal project to take the responsibility for getting a lot of the documents saved and seeing most of them went to the German document archives that were just being established at that time.
There were later trials by German courts.
And they would need all that evidence.
There were a lot more criminals around.
And there were many, many trials afterwards in which he also participated as a German-- member of the German bar.
But there was work to be done-- but I had my assignment, too.
I had to keep up my job.
But I came back to the-- the verdict.
The real work was being done by the judges in this last trial in the winter months, before the verdict.
The 14th of April, 1949 was the final judgment.
It took a couple days in Nuremberg.
And I went down to the final judgment.
And that was interesting.
All the court lawyers gathered together.
General Taylor had them all gathered together.
And then again, the press came in en masse.
It was a finale for the project Nuremberg trials, you know.
And everybody who could get there went back to see it.
It lasted a couple days.
Intelligence.
Typical Army intelligence assignment, that's all.
Well, I must say I did a lot of work, anyway.
He quickly went into law in Germany.
Got admitted to the German bar.
Had a little difficulty at first-- they said, but you're an American.
Give up your American citizenship.
I would never give up my American citizenship, he said.
You think I'm crazy?
Anyway, he had his American citizenship-- and they said, but you can't be a member of the German bar as an American citizen.
He said, why not?
He said, there's nothing-- is there anything in the-- show me something in law.
So they worked, they did a lot of homework, but they couldn't find anything in the German law.

Two or three American lawyers of his situation had found that out-- there's nothing in the law that says that if you pass the German-- if you're a fully qualified German lawyer, and have been a German lawyer, you can ask to go back.
They didn't want him, of course.
They thought competition.
But he really wasn't, I don't think, competition, because he developed his clientele.
He traveled all over America and told America-- and told interested people of their rights to get restitution for their-- the things they had been through.
Many of them had left factories, and businesses, and had endless losses, you know.
And those few that had survived-- no balance between these two figures, values.
But still those few that had survived, could, if they could prove this, this, this, and this, could get a certain restitution from the very well-developed-- developing West German law.
And it has been a wonderful country.
And Dr.
Kempner, many people have written to him and say, they feel he played a role by staying in Germany.
Being brave enough to stay there in Germany.
It sometimes took quite a little bravery, you can imagine.
Nuremberg was not liked and-- anyway.
Gradually, Nuremberg has become quite liked.
Now people are writing their doctorates on thesis concerned with Nuremberg law.
And right now, the Bosnian situation has again awakened a great interest in the Nuremberg Trials.

And also, the Germans have really-- one must have great appreciation, and sympathy, and admiration for the really wonderful political figures who have surfaced since 1949, when the West German Democratic Republic was organized.
They've had, now, I believe it's now their sixth president.
And he is a wonderful man-- a really wonderful man.
He is a former judge who reluctantly gave up his judgeship and went to the difficult job of being president-- Roman Herzog.
And his speeches are world known at Auschwitz, recently, on the 50th anniversary.

He made probably the most eloquent speech that's ever been made, when he said, I am now at Auschwitz-- I'm just talking freely, I don't know the-- it's not an accurate representation of his brief speech.
He said, and the only speech I can make in Auschwitz is silence.
We must be silent when we contemplate Auschwitz.
And I think that-- it's just the people were crying.
People from all over the world were there at that 50th anniversary.
And he meant that so seriously.
And he's not the only one.
The presidents of Germany that preceded him-- Richard von Weizsacker's wonderful speech on, I think, it was May 8th, 1985, impressed the entire world, you know.
When he said, we must contemplate this, more or less-- we must contemplate this terrible Auschwitz problem and Holocaust problem.
We must think about it.
We must all be aware of it.
He made a similar speech after seeing-- what is his name?
Spielberg's wonderful movie.
When he saw that movie, it was at the opening in Frankfurt.
And the press, of course, right away-- what is your comment?
He said, there is almost no comment you can make.
He said, when you see that picture, we must all think very much, and constantly.
We cannot forget Auschwitz.
And so they have been really wonderful political figures and have led the nation in a wonderful way.
And I want to say, as a person spending much of my time in the last years in Germany, the German people all feel very badly about this.
It only gradually comes upon them in its completeness, you know what I mean?
They begin to realize.
I mean, they pushed it away from them after the war and went to work like mad building up a new country from nothing.
And more or less don't bother us-- we're working to rebuild a good Germany.
But now they start writing about it, talking about it.
And the German media work is marvelous that they do.
The research they do into these things.
And we find out that not only Germany, but many parts of Europe collaborated to some extent-- and not too unwillingly-- with Hitler.
It was more convenient.
They didn't protest very much, you know.
And so I think everyone has to feel a little bit with Germany.
And you, in your field now, they're-- they're feeling it so much.
They worry about it.
And they occupy themselves so much with it.
And you, in your particular assignment, would probably be interested to know that two men who recently got a prize, a Dr.
Lettel, living near Philadelphia, who has done a lot of helpful work, humane work for Germany after the war, was given an award.
And also a man from Israel-- a very devout Jewish person from Israel, who has done a great deal of research on war crimes and the Holocaust-- were each awarded the so-called Buber-Rosenzweig Prize.
That's for good work on Christian-Jewish understanding of each other.
And they got this award, and they made a couple Sundays ago, a few Sundays ago they made their thank you speeches in Freiburg-- in Freiburg, where they were given these prizes.
And Dr.
Lettel said, Germany, at first you couldn't deal with them.
They didn't believe any of this.
They didn't believe it all.
I think Nuremberg helped them to believe it, with careful, assiduous documentation and publicity, you know?
And gradually, they believe it.
And at that time, he was trying to befriend them with food packages, and lectures, and exchanges-- arranging exchanges with Quakers.
And he said, I hope that soon, German universities, too, can start having a professor for Holocaust studies.
And of course, that would be quite a step forward.
I was quite surprised to hear this, you know--it struck me a little bit amazing, you know.
But he said that, and the whole German TV audience heard it.
And it's an idea probably, they, too-- American universities-- my own little college has a professor now, who spends quite a time.
He's the Holocaust Studies professor.
Even my little Catholic college there has that.
So I mean, it's spreading throughout the US.
And perhaps it is an export product, strangely enough, from America to Germany.
At least he said that in his speech.
And it's very interesting.
The other man from Israel published an enormous book only in German.
He collected all the Nazi laws against the Jews.
And they include such things-- they went right chronologically from the time Hitler came in.
As things developed, then came the law, or the party regulation.
He makes no distinction, chronologically.
He quoted, 'A Jew may not have a canary bird.' And, of course, a Jew cannot visit a theater.
And all these things, step by step, you know.
And he has this 1,000-page book of all these laws and regulations, chronologically.
With German help, he put this book together-- this man from Israel.
And Dr.
Kempner wrote the forward to it.
And the foreword, the first paragraph of the forward-- unfortunately, I don't know it by heart.
It includes how the Jews were treated in Germany.
The rest of the book has no text.
It has only this research on the regulations.
And the foreword of several pages was written by Robert Kempner.
And the first paragraph of that foreword was excerpted to make a monument that has been put up in the section of Berlin where Dr.
Kempner grew up.
Kempner-- a quotation of Robert Kempner, taken from that book.
The Jew was-- I don't know-- all the step by step things, maybe five or six lines, on stone, engraved on stone.
And the names-- I'm not sure-- was it over 1,700 Jews of that area who were extradited or taken out of Berlin, of that section of Berlin where he grew up.
I don't think his name is on it, except under this quotation.
I haven't seen it, myself, but it's been publicized.
It's a great big monument in stone, black stone, enormous monument, with all the names of all the residents and their addresses.
And I don't know whether it's the date of birth or the date of death of the people of that area.
And what do you think, all those addresses?
How the people at those addresses like that?
You know, there was a lot of protest until the Berlin building minister put that up-- you know, it's an interesting monument.
The canary vogel is in it.
The canary bird.
A Jew could not own a canary bird.
You've stayed in Germany all these years.
Hmm?
Well, the army took us and gave us good, liberal leaves home every year.
We went home every year for several weeks.
My job was always in Europe, yeah.
But I retired several years, many years back.
And then I continued working with Robert Kempner.
We went home, too.
